item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and the use of defenestrate in a non forced way make this one of my favourite scips so far.

defenestrate is a great word this makes me very happy hee hee hee :3c

Thank you very much for the kind comment! I'm glad you enjoyed the read. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The combination of numerology, a scip set in a culture other than American and the use of defenestrate in a non forced way make this one of my favourite scips so far. The subtly of the anomaly and its cause is also just fantastic. One of the few times I wish I could +2 but +1 will have to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed to five blackboxes. Originally was going to be the Hubei province. It's pretty there. Lots of lotus ponds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking over this for my map project, there's no Chinese province with a 4-letter name (in English at least).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! Thanks Kain. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's been quite a lot of "unlucky/cursed" numbers, all starting with 42. As in 42 was the first "cursed" number, rather than always being the beginning the sequence. Or was it 48? I just remember that originally, the article was known as the "Spear of Chaos". Shape changing weaponised gauntlet that caused berserker mode, mild mutation.

That said, I do dig this kind of thing. Feng Shui meets number theory. It would be excellent to put in a campaign somewhere, or even to expand in some sort of story. Good job, have an upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are various ways of interpreting it.

Spoilers
hide

My headcanon is that the guy killed the original tenants and the two people mentioned. He invoked a ritual involving arranging commonplace items and writing numbers in blood—"life became numbers and numbers became life". When he doesn't happen to be wearing his humanoid form and consciousness, he essentially is the numbers on the walls (his blood was needed for the ritual as well). As such, he's bound to the apartment and the carefully-arranged objects.

The reason he keeps his humanoid form is so he can maintain the ritual. The ritual needs blood. The two additional disappearances (the janitor and the landlord) may have been unnecessary, but the ghost is rather aversive to people who don't "understand" numbers…

Of course, that's what was going through my head when I wrote this. You're free to interpret as you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it's thanks to Scantron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no, seriously, it doesn't seem to be killing people, does it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strong #site19 influences here, obviously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the slightly unfamiliar phrasing was the part I liked the most of this. It really crystallized the tone and sold the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for successfully dropping the word "defenestrated" in casual conversation. I quite enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mysteriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mathemancy!

How did the people disappear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I know it's just a coincidence, and the name is quite different from mine in actual hanzi. It's just a bit startling. (you know like the articles that use code to address you by username). And also, because my name isn't anglicized I very rarely run into situations where characters share my name - which exacerbates it a bit. All that said, it's not even a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple answers, which I hope will help:

1. Not really? When I was writing the interview, I took what I wanted 1334-1 to say in English, then I translated it to Mandarin (to my best ability… I'm definitely not as fluent as I used to be) and then I translated it back into English, keeping the Chinese-specific phrases intact. I tried to convey some of the Chinese diction/dialect to keep with the theme of the SCP.

2. …wow, it does. No, it wasn't intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple more questions about this article in order to improve the translation:

  1. Are there any rules about how SCP-1334-1 speaks? He does not seem to avoid using 4 words in a sentence, for example.
  2. “The ceiling is a wall. Five is a lucky number. Every room is safe.” almost sounds like a haiku. Was that intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not only is this incredibly interesting, it's ALSO educational (in numerology at least)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this, for reasons mostly already stated. My favorite thing, I think, is the conversation log. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, it is extremely creepy to have one's name (or a very close approximation) show up in a SCP >.>

If you're referring to Agent Tai Yang Shen, I came up with the name intending for it to translate to "sun god" or "太阳神". It's meant to be an alias, not his real name (also my headcanon is that he uses the name "Apollo" in other countries, as a nod to the Greek god of the sun and music, plagues and medicine, and science).

item.title: Re: Author's Notes II
item.content:encoded: That's… certainly one way to think of it. It wasn't what I thought of originally.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes II
item.content:encoded:
spoiler
– hide block

All agents who encountered SCP-1334-1 reported dizziness and nausea upon leaving the apartment; these symptoms were determined to be due to low red blood cell count. The cause of these effects is unknown.

My take was that he was attempting to make them have a lucky number of blood cells by taking away excess red blood cells.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Random note, I don't know if it was intended or not, but I also like how the SCP was the tenant of the sixth floor. I don't believe this is _actual_ numerology, but the pronunciation of 'six' in Chinese mandarin is _also_ similar to the pronunciation of the mandarin word for 'remain'. (there is a difference in tonal inflection though).

Compare 'six': http://www.hantrainerpro.com/dictionary/chinese-english/translation-liu_six.htm
to 'remain': http://www.hantrainerpro.com/dictionary/chinese-english/translation-liu_remain.htm

Also, Zyn, this was awesome. It made me glee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me to my dad many times: Look, do you want your password to be lucky or secure?

Also, it is extremely creepy to have one's name (or a very close approximation) show up in a SCP >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since incense sticks were originally in groups of three, they'd return to groups of three. The original number supercedes any other arrangement in this case.

The numerical association factors in too—for example, if you're giving a couple a gift, the idea of "double happiness" (囍, "happiness", which is two "喜", "like/fondness" or part of 喜欢, which means like in a crush or potentially romantic sense) might be better than "wealth", so you might give them two of something.

Multiplying the numbers doesn't exactly result in super lucky numbers, because the phonetic pronunciation of the numbers also determines how auspicious they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
arranged in multiples of …

What if one combines 120 sticks of incense into a bundle? It is a multiple of 3, 5, and 8, as well as 4. Which number overrides which?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that people are going on and on about the significance of this article's number. It's just so… fitting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Granted, the unlucky numbers' digits add up to 8 ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it matters? Rice wine isn't the only kind of wine found in China, and the very common baijiu, whose characters 白酒 mean literally "white" and "wine" (the translation is essentially infelicitous, according to Wikipedia) is actually a liquor usually made from sorghum.

It's not a major point in the article, and the reader being free to interpret "wine" however they will shouldn't impact their experience negatively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just me being a little obsessive, but wouldn't it be better to specify the 'wine' in -

pairs of chopsticks, bottles of wine, articles of jewelry, and chinaware arranged in multiples of two

as rice wine?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: This should teach me not to do math before I have coffee. Message deleted because I had the dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More things need to be defenestrated. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The digital ones just show 8:08 and never change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yes, us Asians and our obsession with good luck and longevity.

I still sleep with money under my pillow for Chinese New Year ._.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait… if the clocks aren't working, how does anyone know what the digital ones are set to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent use of numerology. I really liked this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. Upvoted then. Kept the upvote because Chinese numerology.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes II
item.content:encoded: Tautological statements are tautological.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes II
item.content:encoded:
The items in fives represent the five elements of earth, water, metal, fire, and wood, which are associated with the number five.

This was brought to you by the Department of Redundancy Department, which brought this to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Interesting, number-oriented, taught me a few things about a different culture, and taught me the word "defenestrate."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhh yeah. My dad would insist that his car's license plate was lucky because it had the date of his college graduation or the day he came to America or something or other I don't remember anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I distinctly remember my parents being very excited when our new car's license plate had two 8's in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weizhong's right! :D

Also, in regards to the number 8, it sounds like the word for "prosper" or "wealth". There's been a lot of instances of people profiting off of 8s. Here are some random facts from Wikipedia:

- In 2003, A telephone number with all digits being eights was sold for CN¥2.33 million (approximately USD$280,000) to Sichuan Airlines in Chengdu, China
- The opening ceremony of the Summer Olympics in Beijing began on 8/8/08 at 8 seconds and 8 minutes past 8 pm local time (UTC+08).
- As part of grand opening promotions, a Commerce Bank branch in New York's Chinatown raffled off safety deposit box No. 888.
- Similar to the common Western practice of using "9" for price points, it is common to see "8" being used in its place to achieve the same psychological effect. So for example menu prices like $58, $88 are frequently seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely loved the Chinese numerology stuff, since my parents always smashed that into my head when I was younger.

Yeah! I remember making little papercutting (剪纸) fish decorations for family or friends of the family gifts (people put them on windows). My parents would usually ask "oh, could you make one/two/three more? It's a luckier number". Eventually I just started making only two, three, or eight fish at a time for gifts.

Same with flowers in bouquets. Sometimes if there were four flowers in an arrangement, we'd take one out before giving them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but it still sticks out to me simply by virtue of being the go-to 'weird' word.

Incidentally, "defenestrate" can also be defined as "a usually swift dismissal or expulsion (as from a political party or office)", and the origin is de- + Latin fenestra (window). It's apparently been in use since 1620.

also I kind of liked the idea of the Foundation telling an agent "Okay, wear this suit with a specially-tailored thread count. We need you to go into the apartment, open a first story window, and throw out a pair of chopsticks."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderful, and I'm glad this slot has finally been filled. If I were forced to be critical, I would have to say that at times this is a little too subtle, but that's not always a bad thing; the reader still gets a very clear idea of what's going on here. Also, the smallest of things; 'defenestrate'. You have, astonishingly, managed to find a legitimate and mundane use for the word, but it still sticks out to me simply by virtue of being the go-to 'weird' word. It's not a huge deal, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chinese numerology? Excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always am amused when newbies think that something has become the cursed slot when this has happened so many times in the past.

*proudly wears Curse-Breaker medal*

*hands Zyn a Curse-Breaker medal too*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ii liek dis SPC

It's memorable enough that I will think about it long after I have finished reading. This equals success. One thing I liek most which I don't often see is the use of Foundation people apparently local/native to the location of the thing. There's only so many times you can get away with Agent Johnson and Agent Lynch in Chechnya or Italy or southern Louisiana.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely loved the Chinese numerology stuff, since my parents always smashed that into my head when I was younger. I kinda liked the subtle nod at the prior 1334 attempts with the whole theme of luck. I was kinda worried that any successful 1334 entry would try to be way over the top with the whole luck thing, and end up being an 048 copy.

I did feel that the ritual aspect was a little hard to grasp, and I totally missed the fact that he had extended his life through numbers. To me, when you stated that he was missing prior to his 44th birthday, I thought you meant that he had just left, nobody knew where he was, and then he returned. Perhaps you could make it a little more obvious that he's older than would be possible? Maybe a little anachronism?

+1 anyways, because I still liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some clarification on Chinese: (I'm Chinese-American)

4 is considered very unlucky because it's pronounced 四 "sì", which sounds similar to 死 "sí," which means death.

8 is lucky because it's tied to Ba Gua, a Taoist philosophy that states that there are 8 principles of reality and philosophy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always thought that no article could be successful on the 1334 slot, but this proved me otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, she broke it with a humanoid.

I rest my case.

Also, oi, Zyn. This one was beautiful. : ·

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. One of my favorite words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the haunted house, I like the numerology, I like the feeling of a different culture. Good job, Zyn! This was very enjoyable to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "defenestrate."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chinese number superstitions definitely tickles my creepy bone. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoa, I'm still making changes. :3 Added some stuff. You're right; from the standpoint of someone who doesn't have context the handling doesn't make sense. I initially tried to avoid the haunted house cliche by keeping out some details, but in essence this thing is as much a haunted house as it is a giant building-wide ritual site.

I might add another log with Foundation tenants, but I'm concerned about the length. The gist would be that someone who wasn't numerology-savvy would need to be moved out due to blood loss, and someone who was superstitious with numbers would be able to essentially live in the apartment peacefully.
